narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to passing the bar exam as riskier than we can support right now—it's not about doubting your potential, but about patterns from thousands of similar stories that shaped its view. At the top of what concerned the model was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, placing you near the bottom of your class academically during that critical later stage, which was the single strongest signal pulling toward a higher chance of bar failure. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 6.0, showing your grades started middling and trended downward, painting a picture of mounting academic hurdles. Your LSAT score of 21.3 added to that worry, as it fell on the lower side compared to those who typically power through the bar. Coming from a middle-income family also played a role, often meaning fewer resources for the intense prep that bar success demands. Your background as a Black student factored in too, reflecting broader systemic challenges that have tripped up others in similar spots, even among full-time students like you. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.2 nudged things further toward caution, while being full-time was a slight positive amid these headwinds. Interestingly, your gender as a woman was the one clear pull toward success in the model's eyes, a bright spot showing resilience in that lens. Together, these pieces added up to a 55% predicted chance of not passing the bar, a threshold we couldn't overlook for admission. It's a tough combination, but it doesn't define you—many have rewritten their stories by targeting those early grade dips or boosting test prep. Consider retaking the LSAT or bolstering late-stage academics; you've got the drive as a full-time go-getter to shift the odds. We're rooting for your next chapter.
